title: GGUF Models
lesson:
GGUF is a file format for storing a language model so it is easy to run on everyday hardware — your laptop, a Mac, a single graphics card, even a beefy phone. The name stands for GPT-Generated Unified Format. It is the format used by llama.cpp and, through it, by tools like Ollama.

When you download a model that ends in .gguf, you are getting a single self-contained file ready to load and chat with — no Python, no giant framework, no cloud account.

Think of it as the model's PDF

A model trained in a research lab lives as a messy pile of files: weight files, a config, a tokenizer, special-token rules, sometimes split across a dozen shards. That is great for editing the model but awkward for shipping it.

GGUF is like exporting all of that to a single PDF. A PDF bundles the text, the fonts, and the layout into one file that opens the same way on any device. GGUF does the same: it packs the weights, the tokenizer, the chat template, and metadata (facts about the model, like its name, context length, and which special tokens to use) into one file that any compatible runner can open instantly.

Why this format exists

The popular Hugging Face format (safetensors) stores weights in 16-bit and assumes you will load them with PyTorch on a GPU. That is heavy, and most people who want to run a model at home do not have a data-center GPU. GGUF was built for them. Two design choices make it shine:

  • Built-in quantization. Quantization means storing each number with fewer bits to save space — like vacuum-packing clothes so the same items take a quarter of the suitcase. GGUF files come in many quantization levels baked right in, so an 8-billion-parameter model that needs roughly 16 GB in 16-bit can shrink to roughly 5 GB.
  • CPU-first design. GGUF runs well even with no GPU at all, and can split a model between your GPU and ordinary computer memory. So a model too big for your card still runs, just slower.

Reading the cryptic file names

GGUF files have names like llama-3.1-8b-instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf. The part after the model name is the quantization tag, and beginners find it baffling. Here is the decoder:

  • The number is roughly the bits per weight: Q4 means about 4 bits, Q8 about 8 bits.
  • K means a smarter "K-quant" scheme that protects the most important weights.
  • The trailing letter is size-within-the-family: S = small, M = medium, L = large.

So Q4_K_M is "4-bit, K-quant, medium" — the most popular all-rounder. A second analogy: it is like coffee sizes plus roast strength. Q2 is a tiny, watered-down cup (tiny file, noticeably worse answers); Q8 is a full strong mug (big file, near-original quality); Q4_K_M is the medium everyone orders because it is the sweet spot.

TagWhat you get
Q2_KSmallest file, clear quality loss — only for very tight memory
Q4_K_MThe sweet spot — small, fast, quality close to the original
Q5_K_MA bit bigger, a bit sharper — a safe step up
Q8_0Large, almost lossless — when quality matters more than size

How GGUF fits the workflow

You almost never train in GGUF. You train or fine-tune in the normal Hugging Face format (e.g. with LoRA or QLoRA), and only at the end convert to GGUF for fast, lightweight running. GGUF is the delivery box, not the workshop.

The usual path is: merge any adapter into the base model, then run llama.cpp's converter, which reads the model and writes a quantized .gguf file:

# 1) Convert the merged Hugging Face model to a 16-bit GGUF
python convert_hf_to_gguf.py ./my-merged-model --outfile model-f16.gguf

# 2) Quantize it down to the popular 4-bit sweet spot
./llama-quantize model-f16.gguf model-Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M

Now anyone can run that one file. With Ollama it is a single command, and the model loads in seconds on a laptop:

ollama run hf.co/your-name/your-model-Q4_K_M.gguf

💡 GGUF is the standard way to ship a model for local inference and on-device AI. If you have fine-tuned a model and want a friend to run it without a GPU, hand them one .gguf file at Q4_K_M.
mentalModel: GGUF is a model's PDF: one self-contained file that bundles the weights, tokenizer, and chat template, vacuum-packed via quantization so it opens and runs the same way on any laptop, Mac, or single GPU — no framework required.
takeaways: GGUF packs weights, tokenizer, and chat template into one runnable file.
Built for CPUs and single GPUs via llama.cpp and Ollama.
Q4_K_M is the small, near-original sweet spot for most people.
mistakes: Trying to fine-tune a GGUF file directly. GGUF is a delivery format, not a training format. Fine-tune in Hugging Face format with LoRA/QLoRA, merge the adapter, then convert to GGUF at the very end.

Grabbing the smallest quant to save space. Q2_K and Q3 files look tempting because they are tiny, but quality drops sharply. For most uses start at Q4_K_M; only go lower if you genuinely cannot fit it in memory.

Forgetting to merge the adapter before converting. If you trained a LoRA adapter, you must merge it into the base model first. Converting the bare base to GGUF gives you the original model with none of your fine-tuning.

Assuming GGUF runs everywhere LLM-shaped. GGUF is specific to llama.cpp-family runners (llama.cpp, Ollama, LM Studio). High-throughput GPU servers like vLLM prefer safetensors; do not expect one format to fit every serving tool.

Confusing GGUF (the file format) with quantization (the technique). GGUF is the container; the Qx tag is the quantization inside it. A GGUF can even be unquantized 16-bit (F16). The format and the compression level are separate choices.
Ignoring the older GGML format you may still see. GGML was GGUF's predecessor and is now obsolete. Old .bin GGML files often will not load in current llama.cpp — re-download or re-convert to .gguf.
exercise:
Pick a small instruct model on Hugging Face that already has GGUF files published (for example a 1B–3B model). Download two quantization levels of the same model — say Q4_K_M and Q8_0 — and note the file size of each. Run both with Ollama or LM Studio and ask each the same three questions (one factual, one reasoning, one creative). Write down whether you can actually tell the smaller one apart.

Stretch goal: Take a model you fine-tuned yourself (or a base model plus a public LoRA adapter), merge the adapter into the base, then use llama.cpp's convert_hf_to_gguf.py and llama-quantize to produce your own Q4_K_M.gguf. Load it in Ollama and confirm your fine-tuned behaviour survived the conversion.
solution: Hint: Expect the Q8_0 file to be roughly twice the size of Q4_K_M (e.g. for a 3B model, very roughly 3.3 GB vs 1.9 GB). On the three questions you will likely struggle to tell them apart on everyday prompts — that is exactly why Q4_K_M is the default recommendation. Differences usually only show up on hard reasoning, long contexts, or precise factual recall. For the stretch goal, the order matters: merge first (reload the base in full precision, apply the adapter, save), then convert, then quantize. If your fine-tuned behaviour vanished, you almost certainly converted the base before merging the adapter.
summary: GGUF is a single-file format that packs a model's weights, tokenizer, chat template, and metadata together, with quantization built in, so it runs easily on CPUs, Macs, and single GPUs via llama.cpp and Ollama. You train in Hugging Face format and convert to GGUF only for shipping. The Qx_K_M tag tells you the compression level; Q4_K_M is the go-to sweet spot of small size and near-original quality.

keyTerms.def: GPT-Generated Unified Format: a single-file format that bundles a model's weights, tokenizer, chat template, and metadata for easy local running. The successor to GGML.
The code in a GGUF file name (like Q4_K_M) that says how compressed it is: the number is roughly bits per weight, K means a smarter scheme, and S/M/L is size within that family.
The most popular GGUF quantization: about 4 bits per weight using the K-quant scheme at medium size — the sweet spot of small file and near-original quality.
The lightweight C++ engine that created GGUF and runs it efficiently on CPUs and GPUs alike, without needing Python or a heavy framework.
The standard Hugging Face weight format, usually 16-bit and meant for GPU training and serving with PyTorch. You convert from it to GGUF for local running.
Facts about the model stored inside the GGUF file itself — its name, context length, and which special tokens and chat template to use — so a runner needs no extra config.
Storing each number with fewer bits to save space, like vacuum-packing data. A GGUF's Qx tag is exactly this, applied to the model's weights.
GGUF's obsolete predecessor format. Old GGML .bin files often will not load in modern llama.cpp, so re-convert or re-download them as .gguf.
Folding a trained LoRA/QLoRA adapter back into the base model's weights so the fine-tuning is baked in. Required before converting a fine-tuned model to GGUF.

convert to GGUF only to ship and run.</text></svg>
diagram.caption: A multi-file Hugging Face model is converted and quantized into one compact .gguf file that runs locally via llama.cpp or Ollama.

quiz.q: What does a GGUF file actually bundle into a single file?
options: Only the raw model weights, nothing else
The weights plus the tokenizer, chat template, and metadata
The training dataset and the weights
A Python script that downloads the model on demand
quiz.explain: GGUF is self-contained: weights, tokenizer, chat template, and metadata travel in one file, so a runner needs no extra config. It does not include training data or a download script.

quiz.q: You fine-tuned a model with a LoRA adapter and want to ship it as GGUF. What is the correct order?
options: Convert the base to GGUF, then attach the adapter file alongside it
Quantize first, then merge the adapter into the quantized file
Merge the adapter into the base, then convert, then quantize
Train directly in GGUF format so no conversion is needed
quiz.explain: GGUF is a delivery format, not a training format. Merge the adapter into the base weights first, then convert to GGUF and quantize — otherwise your fine-tuning is lost.

quiz.q: A teammate with only a laptop (no GPU) wants to run your fine-tuned model. Which quant should you hand them as a sensible default?
options: Q2_K, because the smallest file is always best
F16, so there is zero quality loss
Q4_K_M, the small, fast, near-original sweet spot
safetensors, since GGUF needs a GPU
quiz.explain: Q4_K_M is the go-to balance of small size and quality close to the original. Q2_K loses noticeable quality, F16 is unnecessarily large, and safetensors is the format you convert away from.

quiz.q: In the file name llama-3.1-8b-instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf, what does the Q4_K_M part mean?
options: The model was trained for 4 epochs on a medium dataset
About 4 bits per weight, K-quant scheme, medium size
Version 4 of the model with a medium context window
Four GPUs are required, with a medium memory budget
quiz.explain: The number is roughly bits per weight (Q4 ≈ 4 bits), K signals the smarter K-quant scheme that protects important weights, and the trailing letter is size-within-family (S/M/L).
